**Glass noodles**, also known as **cellophane noodles**, are a general class of thin noodles made from vegetable starches").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) They should not be confused with rice vermicelli.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The exact characteristics of glass noodles will depend on their ingredients.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The starch used can be derived from mung bean, potato, sweet potato, cassava, canna, arrowroot, and/or pea.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) They are typically transparent to translucent when cooked,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) with a chewy, gelatinous texture and not much flavor.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The color ranges from light brown to white, depending on the starch used to make it.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The noodles typically get all their flavor from other ingredients in the final dish.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)

### Types

[edit | edit source]

* **Dangmyeon (당면):** Korean variety made from sweet potato starch[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)
* **Fensi (粉絲):** Chinese variety, usually from mung bean[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)
* **Harusame (春雨):** Japanese variety made from potato starch[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)
* **Falooda:** South Asian variety made from arrowroot
* **Kyazan:** Burmese variety made from mung bean starch
* **Wun sen (วุ้นเส้น):** Thai variety
* **Bún tào:** Vietnamese variety made from mung bean starch
* **Miến dong:** Vietnamese variety made from canna

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

The noodles are typically sold dried[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) and should be kept in a sealed container away from moisture.

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

Glass noodles must be rehydrated before use. This can be done by a brief period (3–5 minutes) of boiling or a more extended period (~10 minutes) of soaking in warm-to-hot water.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) They cook very quickly, and care must be taken to avoid overcooking, which will make them too soft. They will also absorb a good amount of liquid, so take this into account if adding the dried noodles to something like a soup.

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Dried cellophane noodles are either soaked in warm water or briefly boiled before adding to the final dish. After cooking, they can be used in a variety of soups, salads, stir-fries, dumplings, and other dishes. They may even be deep-fried.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) Conveniently, most glass noodles don't become sticky as they cool, preserving their texture.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)

The noodles are widespread across South, East, and Southeast Asia.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) Chinese cellophane noodles are often used in soups or to stuff dumplings and flatbreads. In Korean cuisine, *dangmyeon* glass noodles are used to make the stir-fried noodle dish called japchae. Japanese *harusame* are often used in salads. Thai *wun sen* are often used in the salad called *yam wun sen* or in the stir-fry dish called *phat wun sen*. In Vietnam, glass noodles are used to make *miến gà*, *miến lươn*, *miến măng vịt*, and *miến cua.* Indian *falooda* noodles are often served with *kulfi* ice cream, where they provide a textural contrast.

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

If you can't get your hands on glass noodles, rice vermicelli can make a reasonable substitute.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)
